does: This paper develops and evaluates two approaches for detecting prompt injection attacks against large language models: one using a pre-trained XLM-RoBERTa model for zero-shot classification, and another employing a fine-tuned variant of the same architecture. The authors construct a test dataset of prompts and systematically compare the classification performance of both approaches to identify which strategy better distinguishes between legitimate instructions and adversarial inputs designed to manipulate model behavior. The core contribution is establishing a baseline evaluation framework that measures detection accuracy across these two methodologies, providing empirical evidence for how fine-tuning impacts the model's ability to recognize prompt injection attempts before they cause harmful outcomes like data leaks or biased responses.
connects: This work builds on growing concerns about adversarial robustness in language models and prompt-based vulnerabilities that have emerged as LLMs gained widespread deployment. It extends prior research on text classification and security detection by applying cross-lingual transformer models (XLM-RoBERTa) to a novel security task. The paper situates itself within the intersection of adversarial machine learning and NLP security, responding to earlier work on jailbreaking and instruction-following vulnerabilities in large models. It leverages supervised fine-tuning approaches that have become standard practice for improving task-specific performance in transformer-based architectures, adapting these techniques to the relatively new problem domain of prompt injection detection.
enables: This detection framework opens pathways toward building more robust LLM application pipelines that can filter malicious inputs before they reach the base model. Success in this direction could support deployment of LLM systems in high-stakes domains where adversarial prompts pose genuine risks. The comparative analysis between zero-shot and fine-tuned approaches provides insights for future work on efficient security mechanisms—whether real-time detection requires task-specific training or whether general-purpose models suffice. Additionally, this work motivates development of larger, more diverse prompt injection datasets and prompts investigation into adversarial training methods that could make models jointly robust to manipulation while maintaining helpfulness.
